coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent Polylactic acid composite incorporating lignin-based carbon quantum dots for sustainable 4D printing applications

Ding, Kejiao
Zhou, Xinyuan
Ji, Tong
Sun, Hao
Chi, Xiang
Wei, Yunzhao
Xu, Min
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent 4D printing materials, as innovative materials that combine fluorescent characteristics with 4D printing technology, have attracted widespread interest and research. In this study, green lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as the fluorescent module, and renewable poly(propylene carbonate) polyurethane (PPCU) was used for toughening. A new low-cost fluorescent polylactic acid (PLA) composite filament for 4D printing was developed using a simple melt extrusion method. The strength of the prepared composite was maintained at 32 MPa, while the elongation at break increased 8-fold (34 % increase), demonstrating excellent shape fixed ratio (∼99 %), recovery ratio (∼92 %), and rapid shape memory recovery speed. The presence of PPCU prevented fluorescence quenching of the CQDs in the PLA matrix, allowing the composite to emit bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The composite exhibited shear thinning behavior and had an ideal melt viscosity for 3D printing. The results obtained demonstrated the versatility of these easy-to-manufacture and low-cost filaments, opening up a novel and convenient method for the preparation of strong, tough, and multifunctional PLA materials, increasing their potential application value.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Crystallinity (χ c) is determined by χ c = (ΔH m – ΔH cc) / (ΔH m o × w i) × 100 %, where w i is the weight fraction of PLA, and ΔH m o is the enthalpy assuming 100 % crystalline PLA homopolymers (93 J g−1).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent Polylactic acid composite incorporating lignin-based carbon quantum dots for sustainable 4D printing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Petrochemistry, Heilongjiang, Academy of Sciences, Harbin, 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Petrochemistry, Heilongjiang Academy of Sciences, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent 4D printing materials, as innovative materials that combine fluorescent characteristics with 4D printing technology, have attracted widespread interest and research. In this study, green lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as the fluorescent module, and renewable poly(propylene carbonate) polyurethane (PPCU) was used for toughening. A new low-cost fluorescent polylactic acid (PLA) composite filament for 4D printing was developed using a simple melt extrusion method. The strength of the prepared composite was maintained at 32 MPa, while the elongation at break increased 8-fold (34 % increase), demonstrating excellent shape fixed ratio (∼99 %), recovery ratio (∼92 %), and rapid shape memory recovery speed. The presence of PPCU prevented fluorescence quenching of the CQDs in the PLA matrix, allowing the composite to emit bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The composite exhibited shear thinning behavior and had an ideal melt viscosity for 3D printing. The results obtained demonstrated the versatility of these easy-to-manufacture and low-cost filaments, opening up a novel and convenient method for the preparation of strong, tough, and multifunctional PLA materials, increasing their potential application value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread application of petroleum-based polymers in industry and daily life, along with the resulting various waste materials, leads to their persistent presence or accumulation in the environment for decades or even hundreds of years, thereby causing severe environmental issues [1,2]. Developing biodegradable polymer materials and promoting their use as alternatives to petroleum-based materials is an effective approach to addressing these challenging environmental issues.

Polylactic acid (PLA) is a bio-based, biodegradable polymer that has gained attention as an ideal alternative to petroleum-based plastics. It is characterized by exceptional strength, biocompatibility, biodegradability, affordability and thermoplasticity [3,4]. Furthermore, PLA is considered a prime candidate for 4D printing due to its favorable shape memory behavior, with its glass transition temperature acting as a switching point. It achieves temporary shape locking and unlocking through amorphous phase transitions, holding significant potential for manufacturing various intelligent structures [5–8]. However, the inherent brittleness of PLA restricts its practical use [9,10]. Numerous strategies have been developed, including blending with various soft polymers or rubbers [11–13], incorporating rigid fillers [14–16], plasticizers [17–19], and chemical copolymerization [20–22] to enhance the overall toughness of PLA. In this regard, blending with diverse flexible/soft polymers represents an economically viable approach [23,24].

In addition to toughness, another key concern is to add more functionalities to PLA to broaden its range of applications. For example, adding flame retardant [25–28], antimicrobial [29,30], conductive [31,32] and fluorescent [33,34] properties to PLA. Among these, the development of fluorescent PLA will expand its use in high value-added materials. The creation of fluorescent composites with controllable emission brightness, excellent processability, and intelligent response advantages could find applications in smart packaging and intelligent labeling, among other fields [35,36]. Furthermore, in 3D printing, aesthetics and functionality are key requirements for adding value to products [37,38]. Fluorescent 4D printed objects could find applications in functional art, offering new perspectives to customers [39,40].

Luminescent materials such as inorganic phosphors, quantum dots, and organic dyes have been incorporated into polymer matrices to impart luminescent properties to materials [41–43]. Inorganic phosphors are complex to synthesize and have expensive starting materials. Organic dyes have low thermal stability, and chemical stability. Quantum dots, as efficient fluorescent nanomaterials, are attractive alternatives to traditional dyes and inorganic phosphors. Nevertheless, they are prone to self-quenching in polymers due to π-π stacking interactions or aggregation phenomena in solid-state [41,44,45]. Therefore, the development of simple and effective methods to prevent self-quenching of quantum dots in polymers is of interest.

In this work, a sustainable composite filament for 4D printing applications with excellent mechanical and fluorescent properties was developed. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were green synthesized from lignin by a simple two-step process. CQDs/PLA/poly(propylene carbonate) polyurethane composites were prepared by one-pot melt extrusion in an efficient and versatile strategy, which can be used to print composites with complex structures and fluorescent properties. The renewable elastomer poly(propylene carbonate) polyurethane could be used to improve the toughness of PLA. More importantly, it facilitated the dispersion of CQDs, suppressed π-π stacking, and unblocked the fluorescence effect of CQDs. The robust and fluorescent composite, characterized by repeatability, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness, is capable of meeting the demands in packaging materials, intelligent materials, and their processing technologies.

Poly(propylene carbonate) polyurethane (PPCU) was purchased from Zhongke Jinlong Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Polylactic acid (PLA, 4032D) was purchased from NatureWorks (USA). Commercial chain extender with epoxy functions (ADR, 4370S, purity 99 %) was purchased from BASF (Germany). Alkali lignin (purity 97 %) was purchased from TCI Chemicals (China). 2,4-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid (purity 97 %) was purchased RHAWN Corporation (China). All these materials were directly used without further purification.

The synthesis of lignin-derived CQDs was based on the method of Lingli [46]. Specifically, 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (0.3 g) and lignin (0.3 g) were added to deionized water (30 mL). The solution was heated in a water bath at 90 °C with constant stirring at 350 rpm for 1 h. After heating, the solution was filtered through the poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) microfiltration membrane with a pore size of 0.8 μm. The resulting filtrate was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated in a temperature-controlled oven at 200 °C for 12 h. The filtrate was then cooled down to room temperature and subsequently filtered again through a microfiltration membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was then dialyzed by bag dialysis in deionized water for 48 h. Finally, the resulting CQDs were collected through freeze-drying.

Both PLA and PPCU were first dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 10 h to remove moisture. PLA, PPCU, CQDs, and ADR were mixed in different ratios (Table S1) and added to a Haake rheometer. In the designation of the samples, PLA-xCQDs, PLA-xPPCU, xPPCU-yCQDs, and xPPCU-yCQDs-zADR, where xyz refers to the weight ratio of the corresponding material. For example, 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR represents the composite prepared from PLA (69.20 wt%), PPCU (30 wt%), CQDs (0.50 wt%) and ADR (0.30 wt%). The mixtures were melt blended in the Haake rheometer at 50 rpm and 180 °C for 15 min. The resulting mixtures were removed from the mixer and cooled down to room temperature. The large blocks of mixtures were then crushed into small granules and pressed into films by hot pressing at 180 °C and 1 MPa for 10 min for further testing.

Pure PLA and composite were selected to make filaments for 3D printing tests. The mixture obtained by melt blending was then added to the 3D printing consumables extruder. The average diameter of the extruded filaments was about 1.75 mm. The mold temperature is set at 160 °C, and the barrel zones are set at 180 °C and 170 °C, respectively. The screw speed was set at 30 rpm. The 3D printing models were 3D printed on a commercial 3D printer (Hori Z400D) fitted with a 0.8 mm diameter nozzle. All 3d-printed samples were printed at printing temperature of 190 °C, bed temperature of 30 °C, layer thickness of 0.3 mm, and printing speed of 50 mm/s, with the fill density and flow density set to 100 %.

The detailed description of the characterization of various properties, including but not limited to mechanical testing, thermal analysis, and structural characterization, can be found in the supplementary section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a showed the schematic diagram of the preparation of CQDs. Lignin served as the carbon source, and the 2,4-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid was used as N and S dopants. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images clearly illustrated that the CQDs were generally spherical and uniformly dispersed in water, with a size distribution ranging from 2.0 to 4.5 nm. The CQDs exhibited bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1b). The UV–vis spectrum exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 280 nm, attributed to the π–π* absorption band associated related to the aromatic sp2 structural region [47,48]. The fluorescence emission spectrum (labeled Em) of the CQDs was shown in Fig. 1c, with the emission wavelength and intensity varying with the excitation wavelength. The CQDs exhibited a maximum emission intensity at 516 nm when excited at a wavelength of 440 nm. Increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 380 nm resulted in a significant enhancement of the emission peak, causing the emission wavelength to shift from the UV region to the visible region, indicating a pronounced red-shift behavior. The intensity of the emission peaks increased further when the excitation wavelength was increased from 380 nm to 440 nm. And the position of the emission peaks was constant, showing a weak excitation dependence, indicating that the CQDs had fewer surface state defects [49].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum revealed that three peaks at 1601, 1495, and 1425 cm−1 were associated with aromatic rings [48]. The C-S/CS vibration peaks, detectable in the range of 600–800 cm−1, indicated effective doping of sulfonic acid groups from 2,4-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid into the CQDs (Fig. S1) [46]. Furthermore, the chemical structure information of the CQDs was investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The survey XPS spectrum showed four prominent peaks at positions 284.8, 532.1, 401.1, and 168.1 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. 1d). The CQDs were primarily composed of carbon, oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s possessed three peaks corresponding to C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C=N (286.1 eV), and CO (289.2 eV) (Fig. 1e). The O 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into peaks related to CO (531.8 eV) and CO (533.2 eV) (Fig. 1f). The N1s spectrum displayed two peaks, NH (401.4 eV) and CN (399.2 eV) (Fig. 1g). The S 2p spectrum could be divided into three peaks at 168.2, 169.3, and 170.4 eV, attributed to the -C-SOx- (x = 2, 3, 4) units derived from sulfonic acid groups (Fig. 1h). These XPS data provide further evidence that CQDs were doped with some heteroatoms (O, N, and S) and had extensively conjugated sp2 domains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical and fluorescent characterization of composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs exhibited effective fluorescence in dilute solutions, but the fluorescence diminished or even disappeared in concentrated solutions or in the solid state [50,51]. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs was attributed to particle interactions that can lead to energy transfer, π-π stacking and charge transfer. These interactions resulted in aggregation induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) of the photoluminescence emission pathways [52,53]. After the CQDs were incorporated into the solid PLA matrix, the material did not exhibit fluorescence effects, and it seemed that the CQDs did not achieve uniform dispersion in the polymer and did not overcome the self-quenching caused by aggregation (Fig. 2a and S2). The clusters produced by the aggregation of the CQDs on the PLA matrix can be seen by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 2f and 2f1). For this purpose, blending with other polymers is a simple and effective strategy to achieve a uniform distribution of the CQDs to unblock the fluorescence effect of CQDs and improve the toughness of the material. Renewable PPCU was selected to keep the material green and sustainable. With the addition of 10 wt% PPCU, the composite exhibited bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, which can effectively solve the self-quenching of the CQDs.

Mechanical properties are crucial in comprehending, designing, and analyzing composite performance. Incorporating the CQDs into the PLA matrix slightly increased the strength and elongation at break of the composite, but did not improve the inherent brittleness of PLA, as the elongation at break remained below 7 % (Fig. S3). PPCU could effectively toughen PLA, which was attributed to the dissipative micromechanism induced by PPCU particles. The elastomer PPCU can act as a stress concentrator in the composite, had the ability to absorb impact, and endowed the material with significant ductility [54,55]. PLA-30PPCU had the elongation at break of 296 % and tensile strength of 33 MPa (Fig. S4). After the addition of CQDs to PLA/PPCU system, the composites maintained their tensile strength while experiencing a significant decrease in elongation at break (Fig. S5 and 2c). The fracture surface of the PLA in the SEM images (Fig. 2e) showed a smooth interface with limited plastic deformation, representing brittle failure. After the addition of PPCU, the rough fracture surface indicated that PPCU could effectively toughen PLA (Fig. 2g). In the PLA/PPCU/CQDs system, better dispersion of CQDs was observed compared to PLA/CQDs (Fig. 2h). This indicated that PPCU could achieve good dispersion of CQDs, thereby unblocking the fluorescence effect of CQDs. The possible reason was that the CQDs have a conjugated carbon skeleton structure, which can interact with the aromatic structure in PPCU. This interaction may have reduced the aggregation of the particles and helped the CQDs disperse more uniformly in the PPCU matrix. Previous studies had found that CQDs could achieve good dispersion in the matrix of Thermoplastic Polyurethane (TPU), which had a similar chemical structure to PPCU, and exhibited fluorescence [56,57]. Furthermore, by observing the microscale morphology of the fracture surface of the composite after tensile failure, the underlying mechanical failure mechanism can be intuitively understood. The PLA-30PPCU exhibited yielding behavior and significant out-of-plane deflection and deformation before failure, effectively dissipating fracture energy and greatly enhancing toughness (Fig. S7a). After the addition of CQDs, the tensile fracture surface of the composite exhibited distinct regions of crack propagation and extensive voids (Fig. S7b). The voids formed during the stretching process have a significant effect on the elongation or ductility of the polymer [28,58]. Nanoparticles can induce phase separation in biphasic polymers under certain conditions [59,60]. Nanoparticles can preferentially associate with one polymer phase over the other, leading to phase exclusion or spatial occupation, which causes polymer chains to repel each other in space, resulting in phase separation. This microphase separation could lead to non-uniform distribution in the interfacial region, reducing interfacial adhesion between polymers. Low interfacial adhesion could lead to premature interfacial failure, resulting in rapid and catastrophic crack propagation.

Based on this, the incorporation of the chain extender ADR was chosen to enhance the interaction between PLA and PPCU at the molecular level, thereby reducing phase separation. This modification could establish cross-linking structures between different phases, prevent easy phase separation during stretching, and thereby improve the mechanical properties. The crack propagation region on the tensile fracture surface of the composite exhibited more significant polymer deformation compared to 30PPCU-0.5CQDs (Fig. S7c). The interconnected network formed between the polymers indicated increased interfacial adhesion, which inhibited crack initiation and propagation, ultimately resulting in a material with higher toughness. Finally, the strength of 30PCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR was maintained at 32 MPa, while the elongation at break increased 8-fold (34 % increase) (Fig. 2d). Importantly, the toughened composite still achieved good dispersion of the CQDs, which exhibited bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2a). Fluorescence spectra showed that the fluorescence behavior of the composite was similar to that of the CQDs, with an optimal fluorescence emission peak centered at 512 nm and an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The position of the emitting peak remained constant, showing an almost excitation-independent behavior (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal property and shape memory behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal behavior of PLA and the composites was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Fig. 3a and b). The glass transition temperature (T g) of PLA was 61.53 °C, the cold crystallization temperature (T cc) was 135.95 °C and the melting point (T m) was 166.50 °C, which were similar to previous reports [61]. The T g of 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR was 57.91 °C, and the incorporation of PPCU and CQDs did not significantly change the T g of the composites. After the addition of CQDs and PPCU, the crystallinity (χc) of the composites increased from 0.49 % to 3.30 % and 4.70 %, respectively. CQDs could act as nucleating agents for PLA. The flexible segments in PPCU increased the mobility of PLA molecular chains, promoting their rearrangement and crystallization, thereby improving the crystallinity of PLA. Under the combined effect of CQDs and PPCU, 30PPCU-0.5CQDs showed the χc of 8.75 %. However, the cross-linking reaction of ADR resulted in the formation of entangled structures between the molecular chains of PLA and PPCU. This cross-linking structure restricted the mobility of the molecular chains, resulting in a reduced crystallization ability of the PLA phase (7.43 %). The onset temperature (T onset) at 5 % weight loss of all materials was above 250 °C, as shown in Table 1 . The T onset of 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR was 287.27 °C, which was higher than the extrusion and 3D printing temperatures of the material, ensuring the viability of the composite for 3D printing and everyday use.

The dynamic mechanical properties of materials contribute to the understanding of shape memory properties, which can predict shape memory behavior and determine the thermo-mechanical conditions for programming shape fixation and recovery processes. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was used to investigate the temperature dependence of the storage modulus and loss factor (tanδ) of the composites. In the low temperature glass region (0–60 °C), the storage modulus of the composites was generally lower than that of PLA, due to the contribution of CQDs fillers, which increase the free volume between particles and matrix, and the flexibility imparted to the material by PPCU (Fig. 3c). In the presence of ADR, 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR showed a higher storage modulus, which could be attributed to the cross-linking reaction of ADR leading to the formation of an entangled structure between PLA molecular chains and PPCU molecular chains in the polymer matrix. Subsequently, the molecular chains in the composites transitioned from the glassy state to the highly elastic state near the T g, the molecules moved violently, and a sharp decrease in the storage modulus was observed. The shape memory effect of the material was based on the loss of modulus near the transition temperature, which allowed the release of stored elastic energy as a mechanical restoring force to drive the shape recovery process [62,63]. The curves of tanδ versus temperature of the composites are shown in Fig. 3d. The addition of PPCU significantly reduced the tanδ values in these composites, resulting in lower damping compared to pure PLA. The flexible PPCU molecular chains could form a network in the composite, acting as elastic elements and resisting the viscous flow of the polymer chains [60,64]. Normally, friction between molecular chains generates heat, consumes the elastic potential energy that should be stored in the molecular chains, and leads to irreversible deformation [65]. The inclusion of PPCU reduced the energy loss during the deformation process of the composites, which was beneficial for shape recovery in the shape memory process.

These thermodynamic results indicated that PLA/PPCU composites were a promising material for shape memory. Fig. 3e showed the shape recovery ratio versus time curves of the composites under 70 °C water bath conditions, and the shape fixed ratio and shape recovery ratio were quantitatively evaluated. T g could be used as a ‘switch’ for shape memory in composites, where physical or chemical cross-links between molecular chains act as the fixed phase to define the permanent shape. The amorphous segments of the polymer chains had better extensibility and deformability at temperatures above T g than at room temperature, which could be used for shape programming. When the temperature dropped below T g, the amorphous polymer chains changed to a glassy state that could lock in the energy, which could not be released without a specific stimulus. When the composites were reheated, the stored deformation strain energy was released and shape recovery was achieved. After the addition of PPCU, the composites were able to recover their initial shape within 15 s under 70 °C water bath conditions, with a much higher shape recovery rate than PLA (54 s). This was consistent with the results observed in the DMA. On the other hand, due to the high storage modulus, all materials exhibited a shape fixed ratio of 99–100 % (Fig. 3f). The network of PPCU molecular chains in the composites provided strong elasticity, and the materials exhibited more than a 90 % shape recovery ratio. The shape memory recovery process of 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR in bending and torsion under 70 °C water bath was shown in Fig. 3g. The higher storage modulus of 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR in the glassy state and lower damping allowed for more energy to be stored during the deformation process, resulting in less energy consumption, and achieving a faster shape recovery speed (9 s) and a higher shape recovery rate (92 %). The composite maintained >99 % shape fixed ratio and > 80 % shape recovery ratio after 10 shape memory cycles (Fig. S8).

The rheological characteristics of multiphase materials are closely correlated with the interactions among their constituents and the morphology of the phases [66]. The rheological behavior of the composites at 190 °C were displayed in Fig. 4a. The storage modulus of PLA exhibited a strong frequency dependence, increasing with increasing frequency. At low frequencies, the polymer molecular chains had sufficient time to untangle, resulting in a significant relaxation phenomenon and a consequent decrease in storage modulus. When the polymer melt was deformed at high frequencies, the molecular chains did not have sufficient time to untangle, resulting in an increase in modulus [67]. The addition of CQDs did not change the rheological behavior of PLA, which had a profile similar to that of PLA, indicating a weak interfacial interaction between PLA and CQDs [68]. The addition of PPCU resulted in a significant increase in the storage modulus of the composites, particularly in the low frequency range. PPCU enhanced the melt elasticity and made it easier for the molecular chains to be entangled in the matrix, indicating that the presence of PPCU effectively inhibited large-scale polymer relaxation. At higher frequencies, the difference in energy storage modulus decreased, and the rheological behavior of the materials was dominated by PLA. CQDs weakened the interfacial bonding between PLA and PPCU, thereby reducing the elastic response of the composites. The introduction of ADR formed a chemical cross-linking network within the polymer matrix, which imposed more constraints on molecular chain migration. 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR exhibited a more elastic behavior compared to 30PPCU-0.5CQDs. For pure PLA, the complex viscosity showed a long plateau at frequencies from 0.1 to 100 rad/s (Fig. 4b). After incorporation of PPCU, the complex viscosity of the composite material increased more significantly in the low frequency range and decreased with increasing frequency, showing that the material exhibited shear thinning behavior in its molten state. The results demonstrated that the shear thinning behavior of the obtained composite was enhanced due to the softening effect of PPCU. The better flowability of 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR composites by extrusion melting reduced the energy consumption for composite preparation and improved the efficiency of the manufacturing process.

The time-temperature superposition and Cox-Merz rule allowed the conversion of dynamic shear viscosity to steady shear viscosity and angular frequency to steady shear rate, resulting in viscosity profiles over a wider range of shear rates (Fig. 4c) [69,70]. Previous studies have reported a range of melt viscosity and shear rate values that ensure optimum 3D printability of ABS at 250 °C. This range suggested that the true shear rate at the 3D printing nozzle should be around 190 s−1 to 3000 s−1, while the possible viscosity should be around 70 Pa∙s to 500 Pa∙s [71,72]. Obviously, the rheological properties of the 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR composite were well matched to this window to demonstrate its 3D printability. The printing process of the 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR filament was shown in Fig. 4d. Through 3D printing technology, complex geometric shapes could be produced to meet personalized customization requirements (Fig. 4e). Both the printed filament and the samples had a fluorescent effect and showed a bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4f and g).

The surface morphology of 3D printing filaments and samples of PLA and 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR was observed by a microscope. Fig. 5a showed the schematic diagrams of the interlayer and surface of the 3D printed samples. The 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR filament and printed samples appeared pale yellow under normal indoor lighting conditions (Fig. 5b). The surface and interlayer morphology of the composites 3D printed samples showed no significant differences compared to pure PLA. They exhibited a smooth, uniform appearance without noticeable defects (Fig. 5c and d). This clearly confirmed that the 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR composite was compatible with 3D printing technology and had good printing characteristics. 30PPCU-0.5CQDs-0.3ADR combined fluorescent properties with 3D printing technology. The printed samples also retained good shape memory properties, allowing them to deform into various complex shapes in a water bath at 70 °C and maintaining temporary shapes at room temperature. When the samples with temporary shapes were immersed in a water bath at 70 °C, they fully recovered to their permanent shape within a few seconds (∼10 s) (Fig. 6a). 4D printing technology represents a significant innovation in manufacturing, enabling the integration of programmable and responsive smart materials during the production process. This advancement opens new avenues for creating sensors and actuators with enhanced functionalities [73,74]. Fig. 6b illustrated a spring design utilizing 4D printing technology and its shape memory process. Through the combination of intricate structural design and smart materials, the spring exhibited variable stiffness after undergoing heating, deformation, and cooling, thereby enhancing efficiency and adaptability in specific applications. Furthermore, the incorporation of fluorescent materials in 4D printing technology imparts self-illumination capabilities to robotic components under certain lighting conditions. This feature is particularly advantageous for operations in low-light environments, as it aids in accurate positioning, navigation, and identification, significantly improving performance in nighttime or dimly lit conditions. Fluorescent 3D printing materials can be used to create products with innovative designs and dynamic appearances. The use of this material provides designers with more opportunities to create more interesting and attractive products.

The aim of this study was to develop a biodegradable fluorescent composite filament with excellent mechanical properties for 4D printing applications. The PLA/PPCU/CQDs composite was obtained through a simple melt extrusion process. Lignin-derived CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment were used to provide fluorescent properties to the composites, and PPCU was used to improve the brittleness of PLA. ADR enabled chemical cross-linking within the composites to achieve better mechanical properties, and the resulting composites exhibited 39 % elongation at break and 32 MPa tensile strength. PPCU achieved good dispersion of CQDs in the PLA matrix, avoiding the typical self-quenching phenomenon of solid-state CQDs and exhibited bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The addition of PPCU improved the processing performance during filament extrusion and 3D printing processes. The excellent rheological behavior resulted in excellent suitability for 3D printing and allowed a wider range of printing speeds. The composite exhibited excellent shape memory performance, with a shape fixed ratio of 99 %, a shape recovery ratio of 92 %, and fast shape memory recovery speed. The fluorescent properties of the material allowed it to fluoresce under UV light, adding visual appeal to the printed structure and creating more innovative and unique products. The shape memory properties could be used to create dynamic shapes that offered novel appearance and functionality. This new fluorescent composite filament combined fluorescent properties with 4D printing technology, offering a number of compelling benefits that opened up a wide range of possibilities for innovative designs and applications.
